primary_diagnosis.name: Acute cellulitis of the right lower limb complicating chronic (secondary) lymphoedema

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic lymphoedema is impaired lymphatic drainage causing progressive protein-rich interstitial fluid accumulation - here secondary lymphoedema following pelvic surgery that disrupted lymphatic channels. Over years the limb becomes massively, asymmetrically swollen with characteristic non-pitting oedema, dermal thickening and hyperkeratosis (a 'peau d'orange' fibrotic skin), and a positive Stemmer's sign (inability to pinch a skin fold at the base of the second toe) - features that distinguish lymphoedema from venous oedema (which is pitting, with varicosities, haemosiderin staining and a negative Stemmer's) and from DVT (acute, deep, diffuse). A lymphoedematous limb has stagnant protein-rich fluid and a compromised skin barrier, making it highly susceptible to recurrent, rapidly-progressive bacterial cellulitis (usually beta-haemolytic streptococci or Staphylococcus aureus). Here a new, sharply-demarcated, hot, erythematous, exquisitely tender plaque with a low-grade fever superimposed on the chronic swelling indicates acute cellulitis. Because the lymphatic compromise predisposes to rapid spread and recurrence, prompt antibiotics and skin care are essential; systemic features (rigors, high fever, toxicity, hypotension) would indicate progression toward sepsis needing intravenous therapy. Cellulitis is a clinical diagnosis; in-person examination is needed to confirm it, mark and monitor the erythema, exclude DVT, and safely prescribe antibiotics. None of this can be reliably done over telehealth, so prompt in-person assessment is required, with clear sepsis/spread safety-netting.

differential.evidence_basis: A new hot, red, demarcated, tender patch with fever on a chronically swollen limb is classic superimposed cellulitis.
Leg change warrants excluding DVT, though a localised surface hot plaque on chronic non-pitting swelling favours cellulitis over a deep clot.
Considered for chronic leg swelling, but absence of varicosities/haemosiderin and a positive lymphatic pattern argue against it.
Can cause red skin, but fever and exquisite tenderness point to bacterial infection rather than allergy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise cellulitis on a lymphoedematous limb, arrange prompt antibiotics/assessment, and actively exclude DVT - without over-anchoring on a clot.

differential.diagnosis: Acute cellulitis complicating chronic secondary lymphoedema (systemically well)
differential.evidence_basis: A demarcated hot tender plaque (DI-001) on a 5-year non-pitting hyperkeratotic lymphoedematous limb with prior cellulitis (DI-002), with low-grade fever but no rigors/toxicity (DI-003), indicates cellulitis suitable for prompt oral antibiotics under in-person assessment.
Should still be excluded on examination/imaging, but the localised surface infection pattern makes it less likely.
No varicosities/haemosiderin; the lymphatic pattern (non-pitting, hyperkeratotic, positive Stemmer's) predominates.
Fever and intense tenderness indicate infection, not allergic dermatitis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is cellulitis on a lymphoedematous limb: prompt in-person assessment for oral antibiotics and DVT exclusion, skin care, and sepsis/spread safety-netting.

differential.diagnosis: Cellulitis complicating chronic lymphoedema - presumptive, pending in-person examination (and DVT exclusion if uncertain)
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation, erythema marking, DVT exclusion and safe antibiotic prescription require in-person assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is cellulitis on a lymphoedematous limb needing prompt in-person antibiotics and DVT exclusion, with clear escalation for spreading infection/sepsis.

red_flags.description: New sharply-demarcated hot, erythematous, exquisitely tender plaque on the shin (cellulitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates acute bacterial cellulitis, which on a lymphoedematous limb can spread rapidly and recur.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated cellulitis progresses to extensive infection, abscess, or sepsis.

red_flags.description: Chronic lymphoedema (compromised, infection-prone limb) with recurrent prior cellulitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Lymphatic compromise predisposes to rapid, recurrent cellulitis and mandates prompt treatment and preventive skin care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to appreciate the vulnerability delays treatment and risks rapid spread.

red_flags.description: Systemic sepsis features (rigors, high spiking fever, vomiting, toxicity) - currently ABSENT but must be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their absence supports oral-antibiotic management; if they develop, the patient needs emergency IV therapy.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If sepsis develops and is not acted on, life-threatening systemic infection ensues.

red_flags.description: Massive non-pitting hyperkeratotic oedema, positive Stemmer's sign, peau d'orange, and Doppler-only palpable pulses on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: These clinician-elicited signs confirm lymphoedema and the limb's vascular status; Stemmer's sign and skin assessment require in-person examination.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires clinician examination; the correct action is prompt in-person assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Any leg change can anchor on DVT; a localised surface hot demarcated plaque with fever on chronic non-pitting swelling is more consistent with cellulitis, though DVT should still be excluded.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-appreciating rapid spread in a lymphatic limb
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Cellulitis in a lymphoedematous limb can progress quickly; prompt antibiotics and monitoring (marking the edge) are essential.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Advising tight compression over the acute inflammation
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Compression over an acutely inflamed painful area worsens pain and is contraindicated until the infection settles.
Cellulitis needs in-person confirmation, DVT exclusion and an appropriate antibiotic choice/dose; self-starting old antibiotics risks mistreatment and masking DVT.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Clinician examination of the erythema margins, warmth and tenderness (and marking the leading edge)
Stemmer's sign and full skin/limb assessment
Exclusion of DVT (clinical assessment +/- Doppler ultrasound)
Assessment of pulses (Doppler) and limb viability
Choice/prescription of an appropriate antibiotic and inflammatory markers if needed
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Prompt in-person assessment (urgent same-day to within 24 hours) to confirm cellulitis, exclude DVT, mark the erythema and start appropriate oral antibiotics; elevate the limb, meticulous skin care, and avoid tight compression over the inflamed area - with emergency department presentation if the redness spreads rapidly beyond the marks, or rigors/high fever/vomiting/systemic upset develop (possible sepsis).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Reassuring or advising unsupervised home antibiotics without in-person assessment risks missing a DVT, mistreating the infection, and allowing rapid progression to sepsis in a compromised limb.
